name: Java Collections Framework Hierarchy
chatResponse: I'll guide you through the architecture of the Java Collections Framework, explaining how interfaces and classes work together to manage groups of objects.

steps.title: Core Interface Hierarchy
steps.content: The framework is built on a hierarchy of interfaces. At the top is `Iterable`, followed by `Collection`. The `Collection` interface is then branched into three main types: `List` (ordered sequences), `Set` (unique elements), and `Queue` (elements processed in a specific order).

steps.title: The Map Interface
steps.content: Interestingly, the `Map` interface is part of the Collections Framework but does **not** extend the `Collection` interface. Maps store key-value pairs and have their own distinct hierarchy, including `SortedMap` and concrete classes like `HashMap`.

steps.title: Concrete Implementations
steps.content:
Interfaces define the behavior, but classes provide the implementation. Common examples include:
- **ArrayList**: Resizable array implementation of the `List` interface.
- **HashSet**: Backed by a hash table, ensuring uniqueness for the `Set` interface.
- **LinkedList**: Implements both `List` and `Deque` using a doubly-linked list structure.

visualCode:
graph TD
    Iterable --> Collection
    Collection --> List
    Collection --> Set
    Collection --> Queue
    
    List --> ArrayList
    List --> LinkedList
    List --> Vector
    
    Set --> HashSet
    Set --> LinkedHashSet
    Set --> SortedSet
    SortedSet --> TreeSet
    
    Queue --> PriorityQueue
    Queue --> Deque
    Deque --> LinkedList
    Deque --> ArrayDeque
    
    Map --> HashMap
    Map --> LinkedHashMap
    Map --> Hashtable
    Map --> SortedMap
    SortedMap --> TreeMap
